---
title: "Ch 1 — Signed Numbers"
subtitle: "Negative numbers as legitimate quantities — and why minus-times-minus is plus"
lesson-id: "ka1.signed-numbers"
tier: "ka1"
kiselev-book: "Algebra, Part I"
kiselev-chapter: 1
kiselev-chapter-title: "Algebraic operations on rational numbers"
kiselev-sections: "§§1–18 (signed numbers and their four operations)"
prerequisites: [t1.negative-numbers, t1.intro-variables]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: [research, ai-ml]
estimated-minutes: 40
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you locate $-3$ on a number line and explain why it is "to the
   left of" $0$? See [`t1.negative-numbers`](../../tier-1-foundations/10-negative-numbers/index.qmd).
2. Can you compute $7 - 12$ and get $-5$ without panicking?
3. Are you comfortable with letters standing for numbers — e.g. could
   you simplify $a + a + a$ to $3a$? See
   [`t1.intro-variables`](../../tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd).
4. Do you accept that **subtraction is addition of the opposite**:
   $7 - 3 = 7 + (-3)$?

If three yeses, proceed.
:::

## Touch

Stand at zero on a long line. The line runs to your right and to your
left. Walking right is positive; walking left is negative.

```{ojs}
viewof a = Inputs.range([-8, 8], {value: 3, step: 1, label: "First step a"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof b = Inputs.range([-8, 8], {value: -5, step: 1, label: "Second step b"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 480, h = 160, padX = 30, padY = 30, midY = h - 50;
  const xMin = -16, xMax = 16;
  const xS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([xMin, xMax]).range([padX, w - padX]);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");

  // axis
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", padX).attr("x2", w - padX)
    .attr("y1", midY).attr("y2", midY).attr("stroke", "#333");
  for (let i = xMin; i <= xMax; i++) {
    const x = xS(i);
    svg.append("line").attr("x1", x).attr("x2", x)
      .attr("y1", midY - 4).attr("y2", midY + 4).attr("stroke", "#333");
    if (i % 4 === 0) {
      svg.append("text").attr("x", x).attr("y", midY + 18)
        .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", 11).text(i);
    }
  }

  // first step: 0 → a (blue)
  const arrow = (x1, x2, y, color) => {
    svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(x1)).attr("x2", xS(x2))
      .attr("y1", y).attr("y2", y).attr("stroke", color).attr("stroke-width", 2);
    const dx = x2 > x1 ? -5 : 5;
    svg.append("path")
      .attr("d", `M ${xS(x2)} ${y} l ${dx} -4 l 0 8 z`)
      .attr("fill", color);
  };
  arrow(0, a, midY - 14, "#26a");
  arrow(a, a + b, midY - 28, "#a26");

  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY)
    .attr("font-size", 13)
    .text(`Walk a = ${a}, then walk b = ${b}. End at a + b = ${a + b}.`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

Two walks. Add their lengths (with sign) — you arrive at the same
spot you would by taking one walk equal to the sum.

## Wonder

1. If you walk $5$ steps right ($+5$) and then $8$ steps left ($-8$),
   where do you end up? Without drawing — which side of zero?
2. *"A debt cancelled is a gain."* Translate that sentence into
   addition of signed numbers.
3. If you take *three* walks of $-4$ each, where do you finish? Write
   that as a multiplication.
4. What could $(-3) \times (-2)$ possibly mean — three walks of
   "$-2$ to the left", reversed?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) is two-step addition. (2) is the reason $a - (-b) = a + b$. (3) is
*positive times negative is negative*. (4) is the famous
*minus-times-minus is plus* — the hardest rule in school algebra,
which Kiselev derives, not declares.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Walks on the line

You walk $+7$ then $-3$. You then walk $-2$ then $-4$. End positions
in each case?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$7 + (-3) = 4$. $(-2) + (-4) = -6$.
*A negative plus a negative is more negative.*
</details>

### Problem 2 — Subtraction as opposite-addition

You owe $\$10$ (think of this as $-10$) and a friend cancels $\$3$ of
the debt. What is your new balance? Write the operation.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"Cancelling $\$3$ of the debt" means *removing* a debt of $3$ — which
is the same as *adding* $3$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(-10) - (-3) = (-10) + 3 = -7$. *You still owe $\$7$.*
The pattern: $a - (-b) = a + b$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — Repeated negative addition

What is $(-4) + (-4) + (-4)$? And by analogy, what is
$3 \times (-4)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-12$ both times. Multiplication by a positive integer is repeated
addition; the sign of the result is the sign of what we are repeating.
**Positive times negative = negative.**
</details>

### Problem 4 — The reflection trick

On the number line, multiplying by $-1$ **reflects** through zero:
$5 \mapsto -5$, $-3 \mapsto +3$. What does multiplying by $-1$ *twice*
do?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Reflect, then reflect again. Where does $5$ end up?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Two reflections cancel: $5 \mapsto -5 \mapsto 5$. So
$(-1) \times (-1) = 1$.
</details>

### Problem 5 — Generalise the reflection

By the same reflection idea, what is $(-3) \times (-2)$? Hint: write
$(-3) \times (-2) = 3 \times (-1) \times (-1) \times 2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3 \cdot 2 = 6$, and the two $-1$'s cancel. So
$(-3) \times (-2) = 6$. **Minus times minus is plus.**
</details>

### Problem 6 — Distributivity test

Compute $(-3) \times (5 + (-5))$ two ways: (a) simplify the bracket
first, then multiply; (b) distribute first, then add.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The bracket is $0$, so (a) gives $0$. For (b), use
$(-3)(5) + (-3)(-5)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
(a) $-3 \times 0 = 0$. (b) $-15 + 15 = 0$. The two agree **only if**
$(-3)(-5) = +15$. So distributivity *forces* the minus-times-minus
rule — this is Kiselev's preferred derivation.
</details>

### Problem 7 — Division as inverse

If $(-3) \times (-2) = 6$, what is $6 \div (-2)$? And $6 \div (-3)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$6 \div (-2) = -3$ and $6 \div (-3) = -2$. Same sign rules as
multiplication: like signs give a positive quotient, opposite signs
give a negative one.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **signed numbers** are the rationals together with their negatives.
The **four operations** on them obey:

- **Addition.** $a + b$ is the result of "walking $a$ then walking
  $b$" on the number line. $a + (-b) = a - b$.
- **Subtraction.** $a - b = a + (-b)$. *Subtraction is addition of the
  opposite.*
- **Multiplication (sign rule).**
  $$(+)(+) = +,\quad (+)(-) = -,\quad (-)(+) = -,\quad (-)(-) = +.$$
- **Division.** $a / b$ is the unique number $q$ such that $b \cdot q
  = a$. The sign rule for division is the same as for multiplication.

:::

A verse:

> *Walk forward, walk back — you add what you take.*
> *A debt cancelled out, is a balance you make.*
> *Like signs make plus when they multiply or divide.*
> *Unlike signs make minus — and distributivity is why.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Compute $\dfrac{(-12) \cdot (-3) - 18}{-3 + (-3)}$.

Numerator: $(-12)(-3) = +36$ (minus times minus). Then $36 - 18 = 18$.

Denominator: $-3 + (-3) = -6$.

Quotient: $18 \div (-6) = -3$. *Unlike signs.*

**Answer: $-3$.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Compute $-7 + 12$ and $12 + (-7)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Both equal $5$. *Addition is commutative even with signs.*
</details>

**W2.** Compute $-6 - 9$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-6 - 9 = -6 + (-9) = -15$. *Two negatives accumulate.*
</details>

**W3.** Compute $(-2)(-3)(-4)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(-2)(-3) = 6$, then $6 \cdot (-4) = -24$. **An odd number of
negatives gives a negative product.**
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Simplify $(-1)^{2026} - (-1)^{2027}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Even exponent of $-1$ is $+1$; odd is $-1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(-1)^{2026} = 1$ (even). $(-1)^{2027} = -1$ (odd).
$1 - (-1) = 1 + 1 = 2$.
</details>

**P2.** Solve for $x$: $-2x - 5 = 7$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Add $5$ to both sides; then divide by $-2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-2x = 12$, so $x = -6$. *Dividing by a negative flips the sign of the
result.*
</details>

**P3.** A bank account starts at $-\$45$ (overdraft). Three deposits
of $\$30$ are made; one withdrawal of $\$20$ follows. Final balance?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-45 + 3 \cdot 30 - 20 = -45 + 90 - 20 = 25$. *Balance: $+\$25$.*
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Show that for any rational $a$, the expression
$a - (-(-a))$ equals $0$. *Why* does it equal zero?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The opposite of the opposite is the original.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-(-a) = a$ (two reflections), so $a - (-(-a)) = a - a = 0$.
The equality $-(-a) = a$ is the reflection-twice argument from
*Discover*, Problem 4.
</details>

**S2.** Prove that if the product of three signed numbers is positive,
then either all three are positive, or exactly two are negative.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each negative factor flips the sign of the product. Track the parity
of the number of negative factors.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Each negative factor multiplies the running product by $-1$. The
final product is $(\text{magnitude}) \cdot (-1)^k$ where $k$ is the
number of negative factors. For a positive result, $(-1)^k = +1$,
i.e. $k$ is even. With three factors, the even possibilities are
$k = 0$ (none negative) and $k = 2$ (two negative).
</details>

### Kiselev-style problems

Three problems in Kiselev's terse classical style, adapted from
*Algebra I*, §§5–10.

**K1.** Compute, paying attention to the order of operations:
$$\bigl[(-3)^2 - 4 \cdot (-2)\bigr] \div (-1 - 4).$$

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$(-3)^2 = 9$, not $-9$. The square neutralises the sign.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Numerator: $9 + 8 = 17$. Denominator: $-5$.
Quotient: $17 \div (-5) = -\dfrac{17}{5}$.
</details>

**K2.** For what values of the rational $a$ does the expression
$$\dfrac{a^2 - 1}{a + 1}$$
equal $0$? For what value is it undefined?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$a^2 - 1 = (a - 1)(a + 1)$, so the fraction simplifies — but only
where the denominator is non-zero.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For $a \ne -1$, the fraction equals $a - 1$, which is $0$ when $a = 1$.
At $a = -1$ the original fraction is $0/0$ — *undefined*.
</details>

**K3.** Without computing each side, decide which of these two
quantities is larger: $(-1)^{99} \cdot 100$ or $(-1)^{100} \cdot (-101)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$(-1)^{99} = -1$ (odd exponent), $(-1)^{100} = +1$ (even).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
LHS = $-100$. RHS = $-101$. Both negative; $-100 > -101$. So
**LHS > RHS**.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**Source pointer.** Adapted from Kiselev, *Алгебра, Ч.1* (1915
edition; modern reprint Fizmatlit 2006), §§5–10. Russian original:
[archive.org scan](https://archive.org/details/1_kiselev_algebra).
Problem K2 mirrors Kiselev's recurrent device of *factor first, then
ask where the simplification is legitimate*.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$-3^2 = 9$."** *No.* Order of operations: the square binds
   tighter than the unary minus, so $-3^2 = -(3^2) = -9$. Compare
   $(-3)^2 = 9$.
2. **"Subtraction is its own thing."** It is not — *subtraction is
   addition of the opposite*. This single rewrite removes most of the
   confusion in signed arithmetic.
3. **"Minus-times-minus is plus is a convention."** It is *forced* by
   the distributive law, as Discover Problem 6 shows. If we had
   chosen $(-1)(-1) = -1$, distributivity would break — and most of
   algebra with it.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you re-derive the minus-times-minus rule from
   distributivity, without notes?
2. Did you solve K1 and K2 with at most one hint each?
3. Can you take a real-world balance / temperature / elevation
   problem and translate it into signed arithmetic?

- **3 yes:** move on.
- **2 yes:** redo Discover Problem 6 — the distributive derivation
  is the heart of the chapter.
- **0–1 yes:** the rules feel arbitrary because the *Discover* steps
  haven't landed. Re-walk them one by one.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`t1.negative-numbers`](../../tier-1-foundations/10-negative-numbers/index.qmd) —
  negative quantities on the number line.
- [`t1.intro-variables`](../../tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd) —
  letters as numbers, the substitution habit.

### What this unlocks

- **`ka1.algebraic-expressions`** *(planned)* — polynomials over the
  signed integers; collecting like terms with signs.
- **`ka1.linear-equations`** *(planned)* — solving $ax + b = 0$ with
  signed $a$ and $b$.
- **`ka2.complex-numbers`** — once the minus-times-minus rule is
  internalised, the question *"what number squared gives $-1$?"*
  becomes well-posed instead of confused.

### Kiselev chapter alignment

- **Algebra, Part I — Chapter 1: Algebraic operations on rational
  numbers (§§1–18).** Russian original on
  [archive.org](https://archive.org/details/1_kiselev_algebra).
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different pedagogical angle):
  [`t1.negative-numbers`](../../tier-1-foundations/10-negative-numbers/index.qmd) —
  negatives as the *Touch* + *Name* idiom (no derivation of
  $(-)(-)=+$ from distributivity, by design).
- **Read alongside the source** for Kiselev's own exercise sets;
  this lesson adapts but does not transcribe.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The minus-times-minus rule is not a quirk — it is a
theorem about every *ring*: any algebraic structure with the
distributive law forces $(-a)(-b) = ab$. The same proof you walked
in Discover Problem 6 generalises to matrices, polynomials, and
modular integers. *One identity, all of algebra.*
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Gradient-descent optimisation moves *opposite* to the
gradient — a multiplication by a negative learning-rate-shaped step.
Reasoning about whether the loss goes up or down is signed-arithmetic
all the way down: $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla L$ is
"$\theta$ plus a negative quantity", precisely the *Touch* walk on a
high-dimensional number line.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Kiselev (the distributive-law derivation of
  $(-)(-)=+$); Pestalozzi (the number-line *Touch*); Mary Everest
  Boole (the *Wonder* questions about debt cancellation).
- **Russian-source citation:** §§5–10 of the 1915 *Elementary Algebra*
  cover signed multiplication exactly in this order. Modern Russian
  editions have repaginated; the section numbering above tracks the
  Fizmatlit 2006 reprint on archive.org.
- **What this lesson does not do:** it does not formalise the rationals
  as a *field*. That belongs to a later, more abstract treatment.
:::
